eli10: Normalization is like keeping your school subjects separate. 1NF: don't cram all homework in one box — one sheet per slot. 2NF: math sheets in math folder, not scattered. 3NF: teacher's room number stays in the teacher table, not copied into every homework sheet.
realWorldExample: Zoho CRM normalized customer → address → country into separate tables (3NF). When India's PIN code system changed, updating the country table propagated everywhere instantly — vs unnormalized, which would require 500k row updates.

question: What is the difference between OLTP and OLAP databases? When would you use each?
summary: OLTP: transactional, normalized, fast writes. OLAP: analytical, denormalized, fast reads of large datasets.
whatTheyTest: Database architecture knowledge and system design decision-making.
explanation: OLTP (Online Transaction Processing): optimized for frequent, short read/write transactions — INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT single records. Highly normalized (3NF+). Row-oriented storage. Many concurrent users. Examples: PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle for order processing, user management, inventory. OLAP (Online Analytical Processing): optimized for complex queries over large historical datasets — aggregations, GROUP BY, multi-table joins across millions/billions of rows. Denormalized (star/snowflake schema). Column-oriented storage (Redshift, BigQuery, Snowflake, ClickHouse). Fewer concurrent users but very large queries. Data is loaded via ETL from OLTP systems.
codeExample:
-- OLTP: normalized tables, point queries
-- Fast for:
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_id = 12345;  -- single row lookup
INSERT INTO orders (user_id, amount) VALUES (42, 999);
UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - 1 WHERE product_id = 7;

-- OLAP (Star Schema): fact + dimension tables
-- fact_sales: sale_id, date_key, product_key, store_key, amount, quantity
-- dim_product: product_key, name, category, brand
-- dim_store: store_key, city, state, region
-- dim_date: date_key, day, month, quarter, year

-- OLAP query (runs on billions of rows in seconds with column store):
SELECT
  dp.category,
  dd.quarter,
  SUM(fs.amount) AS total_revenue
FROM fact_sales fs
JOIN dim_product dp ON fs.product_key = dp.product_key
JOIN dim_date dd ON fs.date_key = dd.date_key
GROUP BY dp.category, dd.quarter
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;
strongAnswerPoints: OLTP: normalized, row-store, optimized for concurrent point transactions. OLAP: denormalized, column-store, optimized for large aggregate scans.
Column storage compresses well and scans only needed columns — 10-100x faster for aggregations.
Mixing OLAP queries in OLTP systems causes table scans that lock out transactional users.
ETL/ELT pipelines (Airflow, Spark) move data from OLTP → OLAP data warehouse for analytics.
whatToAvoid: Don't run heavy analytics queries on your OLTP production database — it locks rows and degrades transactional performance. Always separate analytical workloads to a data warehouse.
ytQuery: OLTP vs OLAP database difference explained
quickAnswer: OLTP: many short read/write transactions, normalized, row-store (PostgreSQL, MySQL). OLAP: complex aggregations over large data, denormalized star schema, column-store (BigQuery, Redshift, ClickHouse). Separate them — OLAP queries kill OLTP performance.
eli10: OLTP is the bank teller — fast at individual transactions, serving many customers at once. OLAP is the bank's end-of-year statistician — doesn't do individual transactions, but can analyze millions of them to find patterns.
realWorldExample: Flipkart runs PostgreSQL (OLTP) for live orders and BigQuery (OLAP) for sales analytics. ETL runs every hour syncing OLTP → BigQuery. Analytics teams query BigQuery — never touching the live database.

question: What is the COALESCE function? How does it differ from NULLIF and ISNULL?
summary: COALESCE: first non-NULL value from list. NULLIF: returns NULL if two values equal. ISNULL: SQL Server NULL replacement.
whatTheyTest: NULL handling in SQL — critical for correct aggregation and reporting.
explanation: COALESCE(val1, val2, ..., valN): returns the first non-NULL value from the list. ANSI SQL standard — works in all major databases. Can take any number of arguments. Used for default values when a column might be NULL. NULLIF(val1, val2): returns NULL if val1 = val2, otherwise returns val1. Useful for avoiding division by zero. ISNULL(val, default): SQL Server specific — returns default if val is NULL (same as COALESCE with 2 args). NVL() is Oracle's equivalent. IS NULL / IS NOT NULL: predicates for NULL checks in WHERE — never use = NULL.
codeExample:
-- COALESCE: first non-NULL value
SELECT
  order_id,
  COALESCE(discount_price, sale_price, regular_price) AS final_price
FROM products;
-- Uses discount_price if not NULL, else sale_price, else regular_price

-- Default for NULL column
SELECT
  name,
  COALESCE(middle_name, 'N/A') AS middle_name
FROM customers;

-- NULLIF: avoid division by zero
SELECT
  total_sales / NULLIF(total_orders, 0) AS avg_order_value
-- If total_orders = 0, NULLIF returns NULL → division becomes NULL (safe, not error)
FROM daily_stats;

-- NULLIF for empty string → NULL
SELECT COALESCE(NULLIF(phone, ''), 'No phone') AS contact
FROM users;

-- IS NULL in WHERE (never use = NULL)
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE shipped_date IS NULL;     -- correct
-- SELECT * FROM orders WHERE shipped_date = NULL;  -- WRONG: never matches

-- NULL in aggregates: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column)
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_rows,          -- counts all rows
  COUNT(email) AS rows_with_email  -- counts non-NULL emails only
FROM users;
strongAnswerPoints: COALESCE short-circuits — stops at the first non-NULL, doesn't evaluate remaining args.
NULLIF is the inverse of COALESCE — returns NULL when you want null, not zero/empty.
NULL = NULL is FALSE in SQL — always use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL for null checks.
COUNT(*) counts all rows; COUNT(column) skips NULLs — know which you need.
whatToAvoid: Never use = NULL in WHERE clause — it never matches anything. Use IS NULL. Don't assume empty string '' and NULL are the same — use NULLIF to convert empty to NULL first.
ytQuery: SQL COALESCE NULLIF NULL handling explained
quickAnswer: COALESCE(a,b,c): returns first non-NULL. NULLIF(a,b): returns NULL if a=b (useful for division-by-zero). IS NULL / IS NOT NULL: correct NULL predicates in WHERE. COUNT(*) counts all rows; COUNT(col) skips NULLs.
eli10: COALESCE is like asking 'what's your first choice of ice cream, or if that's sold out, second choice, or third choice'. NULLIF is like saying 'if the count is zero, pretend it doesn't exist (return null)'.
realWorldExample: Zomato's restaurant rating display uses COALESCE(user_rating, platform_rating, 0) — if a restaurant has no user reviews, fallback to platform rating, then 0. Prevents NULL appearing in the UI.

question: What is a foreign key? What are ON DELETE CASCADE, SET NULL, and RESTRICT?
summary: Foreign key enforces referential integrity. Cascade, Set Null, Restrict control what happens when parent row deleted.
whatTheyTest: Referential integrity knowledge and database constraint design.
explanation: A foreign key enforces that values in a child column must exist in the referenced parent column — referential integrity. Without FKs, you can have orphaned rows (orders without a valid customer). ON DELETE CASCADE: when parent row deleted, all matching child rows auto-deleted. ON DELETE SET NULL: child's FK column set to NULL when parent deleted. ON DELETE SET DEFAULT: child's FK set to a default value. ON DELETE RESTRICT (default): prevents deletion of parent row if child rows exist — throws error. ON DELETE NO ACTION: same as RESTRICT but checked at end of statement. FKs add lookup overhead to every INSERT/UPDATE on child — evaluate for high-write tables.
strongAnswerPoints: RESTRICT (default): blocks parent delete if child rows exist — safest, prevents accidental cascade.
CASCADE: convenient for owned data (order_items → orders). Dangerous for shared entities.
SET NULL: useful for soft deletes — keep the child record but null out the relationship.
FKs require an index on the parent's referenced column (usually the PK) — most DBs auto-create it.
whatToAvoid: Don't use CASCADE on tables where child data is important — a single DELETE on the parent wipes thousands of child rows silently. Don't skip FKs for 'performance' in OLTP — they protect data integrity.
ytQuery: SQL foreign key ON DELETE CASCADE SET NULL RESTRICT
quickAnswer: Foreign key: enforces child value must exist in parent. ON DELETE CASCADE: delete children with parent. SET NULL: null out FK on child. RESTRICT: block parent delete if children exist (default, safest). Use CASCADE for owned data; RESTRICT for shared entities.
eli10: Foreign key is like a library card rule — you can't borrow a book from a branch that doesn't exist. CASCADE is like: if the branch closes, all its borrowed books are auto-returned. RESTRICT is: you can't close a branch while people still have books borrowed from it.
realWorldExample: Freshworks' ticket system: DELETE on contacts cascades to associated notes and activities (owned data). But tickets have customer_id SET NULL on delete — ticket history preserved even after contact removal.

question: What is the difference between a clustered and non-clustered index?
summary: Clustered index: rows stored in index order — one per table. Non-clustered: separate structure with row pointers.
whatTheyTest: Index internals and physical storage understanding.
explanation: Clustered index: determines the physical order of rows in the table — the rows ARE the index. Only ONE clustered index per table. In SQL Server/MySQL InnoDB, the primary key is the clustered index by default. Reading a range (BETWEEN, >, ORDER BY) is very efficient — rows are already in order. In PostgreSQL, there's no traditional clustered index — use CLUSTER command to reorder table data by an index once (not maintained). Non-clustered index: a separate B-Tree structure with index keys pointing to row locations (in InnoDB: to the clustered index key; in heap tables: to row address). Multiple non-clustered indexes per table. Lookups require finding the index entry, then fetching the actual row (unless covered).
codeExample:
-- SQL Server / MySQL InnoDB
-- Primary key = clustered index (data rows in PK order)
CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id INT PRIMARY KEY,  -- clustered: rows stored in order_id sequence
  user_id INT,
  amount DECIMAL,
  created_at DATETIME
);

-- Non-clustered index (separate structure)
CREATE INDEX idx_user ON orders(user_id);  -- separate B-Tree, points to clustered index
CREATE INDEX idx_created ON orders(created_at);

-- Range scan on clustered index (fast — sequential disk I/O)
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_id BETWEEN 1000 AND 2000;

-- Range scan on non-clustered index (may require many heap lookups)
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at > '2024-01-01';
-- Each matching index entry → lookup in clustered index → row fetch

-- PostgreSQL: CLUSTER command (one-time reorder)
CLUSTER orders USING idx_created;  -- physically reorders table by created_at
-- Not maintained — reorder is lost after new INSERTs
strongAnswerPoints: Only ONE clustered index per table — choosing it wisely is the most impactful index decision.
Clustered index excels at range queries — sequential disk I/O vs random for non-clustered.
In MySQL InnoDB: all secondary indexes point to the PK (clustered key), not the row address — secondary + PK column = double lookup.
PostgreSQL uses heap storage by default — all indexes are non-clustered; CLUSTER is a one-time operation.
whatToAvoid: Don't use a high-cardinality random column (UUID) as a clustered index in InnoDB — random inserts fragment the clustered index (page splits) causing severe write performance degradation.
ytQuery: SQL clustered vs non-clustered index difference
quickAnswer: Clustered index: physical row order = index order, one per table (usually PK), fast for ranges. Non-clustered: separate B-Tree with pointers to rows, multiple per table, requires row lookup unless covering. PostgreSQL has no traditional clustered index — CLUSTER is one-time.
eli10: Clustered index is like organising library books alphabetically by title on the shelf — the books ARE the index. Non-clustered is like a card catalogue that says 'Romeo & Juliet is on shelf B7' — the cards are separate from the books.
realWorldExample: PhonePe's payments table uses payment_id as the clustered index. Queries by user_id use a non-clustered index — each match requires a PK lookup. Adding user_id, amount, status as a covering index eliminated the second lookup, halving query time.

question: How do you write a query to get the second highest salary in a table?
summary: Multiple approaches: subquery with NOT IN, LIMIT/OFFSET, DENSE_RANK, or MAX with exclusion.
whatTheyTest: Classic SQL problem — tests knowledge of subqueries, LIMIT, and window functions.
explanation: Several approaches to find the Nth highest salary: 1) Subquery with MAX and NOT IN: MAX(salary) excluding the maximum. 2) LIMIT/OFFSET: sort descending, skip first row. 3) DENSE_RANK() window function: most robust for ties and generalizable to Nth highest. 4) Correlated subquery with COUNT DISTINCT. Window function approach is preferred in interviews — it generalises to any N and handles ties correctly.
codeExample:
-- Method 1: Subquery with MAX excluding highest
SELECT MAX(salary) AS second_highest
FROM employees
WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);

-- Method 2: LIMIT OFFSET (MySQL / PostgreSQL)
SELECT DISTINCT salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1;  -- skip top 1, take next 1

-- Method 3: DENSE_RANK (best — handles ties, generalizable)
SELECT salary AS second_highest
FROM (
  SELECT salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk
  FROM employees
) ranked
WHERE rnk = 2
LIMIT 1;

-- Generalize to Nth highest: change rnk = N
-- Second highest per department:
SELECT department, salary
FROM (
  SELECT department, salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk
  FROM employees
) ranked
WHERE rnk = 2;

-- Handle case where second highest doesn't exist
SELECT COALESCE(
  (SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1),
  NULL
) AS second_highest;
strongAnswerPoints: DENSE_RANK() is the interview gold standard — handles ties and generalises to Nth highest.
LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1 is clean for simple cases but needs DISTINCT for tied salaries.
Subquery with MAX is intuitive but requires modification for 3rd, 4th highest — doesn't scale.
For second highest per group, DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept) is the cleanest solution.
whatToAvoid: Don't use RANK() — it skips numbers on ties, so rank=2 may not exist even if a second distinct salary does. Use DENSE_RANK().
ytQuery: SQL second highest salary query multiple methods
quickAnswer: 3 ways: 1) MAX(salary) WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary)). 2) SELECT DISTINCT salary ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1. 3) DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) = 2 (best — handles ties, generalizable to Nth).
eli10: Finding second highest salary is like finding the silver medal winner. Method 1: who ran fastest, but not as fast as the gold medallist? Method 2: line everyone up by speed, skip the first person, pick the next. Method 3: give everyone a rank number (dense_rank), pick whoever has rank 2.
realWorldExample: Common interview question at TCS, Infosys, Amazon. In a real system, this would surface in compensation analytics: 'which is the salary band just below the top earner in each department' — answered cleanly with DENSE_RANK() PARTITION BY department.

question: What are some common SQL performance anti-patterns and how do you fix them?
summary: SELECT *, N+1 queries, functions on indexed columns, implicit conversions, missing indexes — all kill performance.
whatTheyTest: Real-world SQL engineering judgment and performance awareness.
explanation: Common anti-patterns: 1) SELECT *: fetches all columns, wastes bandwidth, breaks if schema changes. 2) N+1 queries: 1 query to get N rows, then N individual queries for related data — use JOINs or batch IN. 3) Function on indexed column: WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2024 prevents index use. 4) LIKE '%prefix': leading wildcard causes full scan. 5) Implicit type conversion: WHERE user_id = '42' (string) when user_id is INT — may bypass index. 6) No index on JOIN columns. 7) SELECT DISTINCT to hide missing JOINs. 8) Unbounded queries without LIMIT. 9) Long transactions holding locks.
strongAnswerPoints: SELECT *: always specify columns — saves network I/O and prevents breakage on schema changes.
N+1: most common ORM anti-pattern — use eager loading (JOINs) or IN clause batching.
Functions on indexed columns prevent index use — rewrite as range conditions on the raw column.
EXPLAIN ANALYZE is your first tool for diagnosing all of the above.
whatToAvoid: Don't assume slow queries need more hardware — identify and fix the anti-pattern first. Don't blindly add indexes — measure with EXPLAIN before and after.
ytQuery: SQL performance anti-patterns N+1 SELECT star index optimization
quickAnswer: Top anti-patterns: SELECT * (fetch only needed cols), N+1 queries (use JOINs), function on indexed col (use range predicate), leading LIKE wildcard (use full-text), no LIMIT (always paginate), implicit type casting (match types to index). Diagnose with EXPLAIN ANALYZE.
eli10: SQL anti-patterns are bad habits. SELECT * is ordering everything on the menu when you only want pizza. N+1 is calling the restaurant 100 times for one order each, instead of placing one big order. Function on indexed column is looking up someone in an alphabetical list using their nickname instead of their real name.
realWorldExample: A CRED API serving transaction history was timing out. EXPLAIN revealed SELECT * fetching a 5MB blob column on every request, a missing index on user_id, and a correlated subquery running per row. Three targeted fixes: SELECT specific columns, add index, replace subquery with window function — 95% latency reduction.
